practice.name: Harvard-MIT Mathematics Tournament #0i9l
practice.statement:
Problem:

A partition of a number $n$ is a sequence of positive integers, arranged in descending order, whose sum is $n$. For example, $n=4$ has 5 partitions: $1+1+1+1=2+1+1=2+2=3+1=4$. Given two different partitions of the same number, $n=a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k}=b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+b_{l}$, where $k \leq l$, the first partition is said to dominate the second if all of the following inequalities hold:
$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{1} & \geq b_{1} ; \\
a_{1}+a_{2} & \geq b_{1}+b_{2} ; \\
a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3} & \geq b_{1}+b_{2}+b_{3} ; \\
& \vdots \\
a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots+a_{k} & \geq b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+b_{k} .
\end{aligned}
$$
Find as many partitions of the number $n=20$ as possible such that none of the partitions dominates any other. Your score will be the number of partitions you find. If you make a mistake and one of your partitions does dominate another, your score is the largest $m$ such that the first $m$ partitions you list constitute a valid answer.

solutionReveal.markdown:
We multiply both sides of the desired inequality by $\sum_{\text{cyc}} x = (x + y + z)$; that is, we are going to show that
$$
\left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot L = (x + y + z) \cdot L \geq (x + y + z) \cdot R = \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot R
$$
or
$$
\left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot L - \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot R \geq 0.
$$
Note that
$$
\begin{aligned} \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) L &= \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^4 + z^4) + 2x^2y^2z^2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \\ &= \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^4 + z^4)\right) + 2x^2y^2z^2 \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right)^2 \\ &= \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^5(y^3 + z^3) + 2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4 y^4 + xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^2 + z^2) + 2x^2y^2z^2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^2 + 4x^2y^2z^2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy \end{aligned}
$$
and
$$
\begin{aligned} \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) R &= \left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) \cdot \left( xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy(x^2 + y^2) + 2xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^2 y^2 \right) \\ &= xyz \left( \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y + z) + \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^2 + z^2) + 2xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^2 + 2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^2 + z^2) + 4xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy \right). \end{aligned}
$$
It follows that
$$
\left(\sum_{\text{cyc}} x\right) (L - R) = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^5(y^3 + z^3 - yz(y + z)) + 2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4 y^4 + 2x^2y^2z^2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy - 2xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^2 + z^2).
$$
We complete our proof by showing the above expression is always nonnegative. Indeed, it is the sum of the two non-negative expressions
$$
S_1 = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^5(y^3 + z^3 - yz(y+z))
$$
and
$$
S_2 = 2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4 y^4 + 2x^2 y^2 z^2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy - 2xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^2 + z^2).
$$
The first expression $S_1$ is clearly nonnegative because
$$
3(y^3 + z^3) = (2y^3 + z^3) + (y^3 + 2z^3) \geq 3y^2z + 3yz^2 = 3yz(y + z),
$$
by the AM-GM inequality. We use the Schur's inequality to establish the fact that $S_2 \geq 0$. Indeed, setting $a = xy, b = yz, c = zx$, we have
$$
S_2 = 2 \left( \sum_{\text{cyc}} a^4 + abc \sum_{\text{cyc}} a - \sum_{\text{cyc}} a^3(b+c) \right) = 2 \sum_{\text{cyc}} a^2(a-b)(a-c) \geq 0,
$$
by Schur's inequality for $n = 2$.

(By Zuming Feng and Xiaosheng Mu). Note that
$$
L - R = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^4 + z^4) - xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy(x^2 + y^2) + xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} (2x^2yz - 2x^2y^2).
$$
We have
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_3 &= \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(y^4 + z^4) - xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} xy(x^2 + y^2) = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y^3 + z^3) - \sum_{\text{cyc}} (x^4y^2 + x^2y^4)z \\
&= \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y^3 + z^3) - \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y^2z + yz^2) = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y^3 + z^3 - y^2z - yz^2) \\
&= \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y^3 + z^3 - yz(y+z)) = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y+z)(y-z)^2
\end{aligned}
$$
and
$$
S_4 = xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} (2x^2yz - 2x^2y^2) = -xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} (xy - xz)^2 = -xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^2(y-z)^2.
$$
Therefore,
$$
L - R = S_3 + S_4 = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^4(y+z)(y-z)^2 - xyz \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^2(y-z)^2 = \sum_{\text{cyc}} x^3(xy + xz - yz)(y-z)^2.
$$
To show that $L - R \geq 0$, we use a method similar to the proof of Schur's inequality. Note that the desired inequality is symmetric with respect to $x, y, z$, so we may assume that $x \geq y \geq z > 0$. We consider the three summands
$$
s_1 = x^3(xy + xz - yz)(y-z)^2, \quad s_2 = y^3(yz + yx - zx)(z-x)^2, \quad s_3 = z^3(zx + zy - xy)(x-y)^2
$$
in our expression for $L - R$. The first is clearly non-negative because
$$
s_1 = x^3(xy + xz - yz)(y-z)^2 \geq x^3(xz - yz)(y-z)^2 = x^3z(y-z)^2(x-y).
$$
On the other hand, the sum of the other two summands is also non-negative because $y^3 \ge z^3$, $(z-x)^2 = (x-z)^2 \ge (x-y)^2$, and consequently
$$
\begin{aligned}
s_2 + s_3 &= y^3(yz + yx - zx)(z-x)^2 + z^3(zx + zy - xy)(x-y)^2 \\
&\geq z^3(x-y)^2(yz + yx - zx + zx + zy - xy) = 2yz^4(x-y)^2 \geq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$
Here the first inequality follows from the fact that $yz + yx - zx \ge 0$. Hence $L - R = s_1 + s_2 + s_3 \ge 0$, completing our proof.

practice.name: Harvard-MIT Mathematics Tournament #0iyq
practice.statement:
Problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{4} + 14 x^{3} + 52 x^{2} + 56 x + 16$. Let $z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}, z_{4}$ be the four roots of $f$. Find the smallest possible value of $\left|z_{a} z_{b} + z_{c} z_{d}\right|$ where $\{a, b, c, d\} = \{1,2,3,4\}$.

solutionReveal.markdown:
Solution:
Note that $\frac{1}{16} f(2x) = x^{4} + 7x^{3} + 13x^{2} + 7x + 1$. Because the coefficients of this polynomial are symmetric, if $r$ is a root of $f(x)$ then $\frac{4}{r}$ is as well. Further, $f(-1) = -1$ and $f(-2) = 16$ so $f(x)$ has two distinct roots on $(-2,0)$ and two more roots on $(-\infty,-2)$. Now, if $\sigma$ is a permutation of $\{1,2,3,4\}$:
$$
\left|z_{\sigma(1)} z_{\sigma(2)} + z_{\sigma(3)} z_{\sigma(4)}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\left(z_{\sigma(1)} z_{\sigma(2)} + z_{\sigma(3)} z_{\sigma(4)} + z_{\sigma(4)} z_{\sigma(3)} + z_{\sigma(2)} z_{\sigma(1)}\right)
$$
Let the roots be ordered $z_{1} \leq z_{2} \leq z_{3} \leq z_{4}$, then by rearrangement the last expression is at least:
$$
\frac{1}{2}\left(z_{1} z_{4} + z_{2} z_{3} + z_{3} z_{2} + z_{4} z_{1}\right)
$$
Since the roots come in pairs $z_{1} z_{4} = z_{2} z_{3} = 4$, our expression is minimized when $\sigma(1) = 1, \sigma(2) = 4, \sigma(3) = 3, \sigma(4) = 2$ and its minimum value is $8$.

practice.name: Berkeley Math Circle #0jhi
practice.statement:
Problem:
Show that for all real numbers $a, b, c$,
$$
a^{6} + b^{6} + c^{6} - 3 a^{2} b^{2} c^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2}(a-b)^{2}(b-c)^{2}(c-a)^{2}
$$
